steps.title: Pin down what 'accelerated germination' requires
steps.content: The blank must give an example of accelerated germination. Acceleration is a comparison: at the same time point, H₂S-treated seeds must reach a higher germination percentage than untreated seeds. A correct choice therefore needs (a) a single time point and (b) treated vs. untreated numbers showing treated ahead.

steps.title: Match the data to that requirement
steps.content: At 48 hours, the graph shows seeds treated with 10 µmol/L of H₂S above 70% germination while untreated seeds sit around 50%. Same time point, treated clearly ahead — that is acceleration.

steps.title: Eliminate the other choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — at 24 hours, less than 10% of seeds treated with H₂S at a concentration of 10 micromoles per liter had germinated, whereas more than 90% of those seeds had germinated at 168 hours.: compares the treated group to itself at two different times. No untreated comparison, so it shows growth over time, not acceleration relative to untreated. ✗
  • Choice B — at 48 hours, more than 70% of seeds treated with H₂S at a concentration of 10 micromoles per liter had germinated, whereas only approximately 50% of untreated seeds had germinated.: same time point, treated ahead of untreated — this is acceleration. ✓
  • Choice C — at 168 hours, more than 90% of seeds treated with H₂S at concentrations of 10 or 500 micromoles per liter had germinated, whereas less than 70% of untreated seeds had germinated.: 168 hours is the endpoint where germination has plateaued; a higher final total shows greater overall germination, not that treated seeds got there faster. ✗
  • Choice D — at 48 hours, approximately 50% of seeds treated with H₂S at a concentration of 10 micromoles per liter had germinated, whereas only approximately 30% of untreated seeds had germinated.: misreads the graph — the 10 µmol/L curve is well above 50% at 48 hours, and untreated is near 50%, not 30%. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
